data.title: Tesla found to be 33% guilty in fatal Florida autopilot crash - update
data.description:
A Florida jury has found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) partly responsible for a fatal 2019 crash, claiming flaws in the company’s autopilot feature were to blame for the accident.

This was the first federal lawsuit involving an accident tied to autopilot to go to trial. The verdict found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) 33% responsible for the crash and the driver of the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), George Brian McGee, 66% responsible. As a result, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) will only have to pay a third of the $200M awarded in punitive damages and $129M in compensatory damages to the two individuals struck by McGee.

The company was sued by the family of 22-year-old college student Naibel Benavides, a pedestrian struck by the Tesla (TSLA). According to details submitted during the Miami trial, McGee reached for his dropped cell phone with Tesla’s (TSLA) autopilot activated while he drove through an intersection in Key Largo, Florida at 50 mph, crashing into a parked SUV on the side of the road. Benavides and her boyfriend, Dillon Angulo were standing next to the SUV when it was struck by the Tesla (TSLA), killing Benavides and injuring Angulo.

During the trial, McGee said his foot was on the accelerator while crossing the intersection, thereby overriding a function of autopilot that would stop for objects in the road. Attorneys for the plaintiff claim Tesla’s (TSLA) misinformation about the capabilities of autopilot created a false sense of security for the driver. Video from the vehicle shows that autopilot recognized the SUV but did nothing to avoid it. Tesla (TSLA) lawyers argued, however, that McGee was a “reckless” and “aggressive” driver going well over the posted speed limit, citing warnings from the Tesla’s (TSLA) car manual that it is “the drivers’ responsibility to stay alert, drive safely, and be in control of the vehicle,” all of which McGee disregarded.

“No car would have prevented the crash,” Tesla’s (TSLA) attorney Joel Smith said to the jury.

The verdict has not had a material impact on Tesla (TSLA) shares in afternoon trading.

Update: Tesla will reportedly appeal the jury’s verdict over its liability in the 2019 crash, though the company has not yet confirmed the move to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Omega Healthcare raises 2025 AFFO guidance to $3.04–$3.07 per share while signaling robust acquisition pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO C. Taylor Pickett reported "second quarter adjusted funds from operations of $0.77 per share and FAD, funds available for distribution, of $0.74 per share reflects strong revenue and EBITDA growth principally fueled by acquisitions and active portfolio management." Management raised and narrowed 2025 AFFO guidance to $3.04–$3.07 per share, citing "strong second quarter 2025 earnings and the issuance of $600 million in 5-year bonds versus the continued sale of equity." Pickett stated, "Our balance sheet metrics are very strong with adjusted annualized EBITDA of nearly $1.2 billion and net funded debt of only $4.3 billion."

  • Pickett highlighted a major event: "In July, Genesis filed a Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Omega, along with other Genesis lenders has committed to debtor-in-possession financing. And in addition, to support a bid to buy assets via Section 363 bankruptcy sale process. In the interim, we expect to receive our full monthly contractual rent."

  • The senior housing portfolio now includes 396 facilities, constituting 38% of the total operating facility portfolio. Pickett emphasized, "With our strong acquisition pipeline, a favorable operating environment and over $2 billion in liquidity with very low leverage, we are ideally positioned to grow both our senior housing and skilled nursing portfolios."

  • CFO Robert O. Stephenson stated, "Revenue for the second quarter was $283 million, compared to $253 million for the second quarter of 2024. The year-over-year increase is primarily the result of the timing and impact of revenue from new investments completed throughout 2024 and 2025, operator restructurings and transitions and annual escalators, partially offset by asset sales completed during that same time period."

Outlook

  • Omega raised and narrowed its full year adjusted FFO guidance to a range between $3.04 to $3.07 per share. Stephenson explained, "The increase was primarily due to several factors. One, we completed $183 million of new investments post our first quarter earnings call. Two, our prior guidance assumed we would issue equity or have approximately $600 million of cash on hand to repay our $600 million of notes due January 2026. We were able to issue bonds versus equity to put the cash on the balance sheet to handle that maturity."

  • Key assumptions for the revised guidance include $3.6 million of monthly revenue related to Avardis, assuming Genesis pays rent and interest pursuant to terms of the debt financing agreement, and Maplewood continues to pay at its July monthly run rate of $6.1 million. No additional new investments beyond June 30 are included in the outlook.

Financial Results

  • Omega reported second quarter revenue of $283 million and net income of $140 million or $0.46 per share. Nareit FFO for the quarter was $213 million or $0.70 per share, adjusted FFO was $232 million or $0.77 per share, and FAD was $223 million or $0.74 per share. Stephenson noted, "Our second quarter 2025 FAD was $0.021 greater than our first quarter 2025 FAD, with the increase primarily resulting from incremental revenue related to the timing and completion of $605 million in new investments completed during the first half of 2025."

  • Maplewood paid $17.6 million in rent during the second quarter, with increases attributed to additional rent from the Washington, D.C. facility and higher rental income from U.K. operators due to favorable currency fluctuations. Omega ended the quarter with $734 million in cash, and 95% of its $5 billion in debt was at fixed rates. The fixed charge coverage ratio was 5.4x, and net funded debt to annualized adjusted normalized EBITDA was 3.67x.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Hughes, Raymond James: Asked about the investment pipeline yields and Maplewood rent trajectory. Vikas Gupta described a "strong" pipeline and stated, "Yield, we continue to push 10% across the board for all of those asset classes." Stephenson added, "They paid $6.1 million most recently. That's what we model on a go-forward basis. My upside guidance would be based on any additional rent, and we're hoping they do pay that."

  • William John Kilichowski, Wells Fargo: Queried strategy shift on the balance sheet and use of equity vs. debt. Stephenson responded, "We have an equity currency...we saw an opportunity and opportunistically took advantage of that. So that's why on the guidance side, instead of having issuing equity to fund the next year's bonds, we decided to do debt for debt."

  • Seth Eugene Bergey, Citi: Asked about Maplewood lease structure and rent upside. Gupta explained, "The lease is structured -- the contractual rent is $69 million, but at this point, we're not looking at it that way. We're looking at all cash flow that comes to Omega."

  • Additional questions centered on tenant EBITDAR coverage, labor costs, dividend payout ratio, Genesis bankruptcy risks, and the composition of the acquisition pipeline.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on guidance drivers, rent coverage improvements, and potential dividend increases, reflecting a slightly positive but cautious tone. Questions frequently addressed sustainability and upside of recent performance improvements.

  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone, particularly in addressing strategy, tenant risks, and growth. Phrases such as "we are ideally positioned" and "we expect our overall coverages will continue to grow" were common. In Q&A, management continued to project confidence, occasionally using language such as "we're just hoping for further improvement" (Gupta) and "we feel really good about the assumption and exit with our portfolio intact" (Pickett).

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more upbeat, with fewer references to uncertainty or external risks, while analysts maintained a constructive but probing stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised and narrowed AFFO guidance again, from $2.95–$3.01 per share in Q1 to $3.04–$3.07 per share in Q2.

  • Notable strategic shift included favoring bond issuance over equity sales to manage upcoming debt maturities, with Stephenson highlighting this as "leverage neutral."

  • Genesis transitioned from liquidity concerns and missed rent in Q1 to filing for bankruptcy in Q2, but management expressed confidence in rental streams and collateral.

  • The acquisition pipeline and transaction activity expanded, with $605 million in new investments year-to-date versus $457 million in Q1. Portfolio mix saw senior housing grow to 38% of the total.

  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism around regulatory and tenant risks in Q1 to greater confidence in Q2, while analysts continued to focus on rent coverage, tenant health, and growth sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Genesis’ bankruptcy was identified as a risk, but management emphasized secured loans, ongoing rent payments, and debtor-in-possession financing as mitigants.

  • Legislative changes, particularly the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, could impact Medicaid funding, but Krull noted, "a reduction in the overall federal funding of Medicaid to the states...may cause states to evaluate all programs."

  • Wage inflation and staffing remain concerns, yet Krull reported that wage increases are "just seeing normal inflationary increases at this point."

  • Currency fluctuations from U.K. investments are being managed with derivatives.

Final Takeaway

Omega Healthcare Investors reported strong second quarter results, highlighted by increased AFFO guidance, robust acquisition activity, and disciplined capital management. The company’s leadership emphasized stability and growth opportunities despite tenant challenges, notably Genesis’ bankruptcy, while maintaining confidence in rent streams and secured positions. Strategic focus on portfolio diversification, especially in senior housing and the U.K., positions Omega for continued growth, with management signaling optimism for the remainder of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sun Country outlines $1.5B revenue and $2.50 EPS targets by Q2 2027 as cargo fleet expansion impacts near-term margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sun Country Airlines (SNCY) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jude I. Bricker emphasized the company's unique, diversified business model, noting "our diverse business model is unique in the airline industry" and highlighted a "12th consecutive quarter of profitability." Bricker stated, "The theme in 2025 for Sun Country is about growth in our cargo business," projecting all 8 new cargo aircraft to be in service by the end of August, bringing the cargo fleet to 20 aircraft. He shared expectations that "fleet growth, along with contractual rate increases will roughly double versus prior contract our cargo revenue once these additional aircraft reach mature utilization." Bricker advised the current reduction in scheduled service volumes should be "recovered as we move through 2026" and projected, "I expect the business to deliver roughly $1.5 billion in revenue, $300 million in EBITDA and $2.50 in EPS" by the second quarter of 2027, contingent on fleet induction and pilot upgrades.
  • Bricker highlighted a year-over-year TRASM improvement of 3.5%, with May showing a 6.6% increase, and noted, "the peak summer months of June, July and August could absorb much more capacity than we were able to deliver with little falloff in unit revenue performance."
  • Interim CFO William Trousdale reported, "the second quarter marked our 12th consecutive quarter of profitability," with total revenue of $263.6 million, a 3.6% increase from Q2 2024 on a 0.5% decrease in block hours. Trousdale noted adjusted pretax margin of 3.9%. He explained, "our cargo block hours were lower than we had anticipated...due to the timing of cargo aircraft deliveries" but highlighted the ability to offset this with increased charter revenue. Trousdale specified, "Revenue in our cargo segment grew 36.8% in Q2 to $34.8 million. This was the highest quarterly cargo revenue in our history."

Outlook

  • Management expects third quarter total revenue between $250 million and $260 million, with block hours up 5% to 8%. Scheduled service ASMs are expected to contract between 9% and 10% in Q3 as the company continues to prioritize cargo. Bricker projected, "With current demand for our product and the current fuel prices, I expect the business to deliver roughly $1.5 billion in revenue, $300 million in EBITDA and $2.50 in EPS" by Q2 2027.
  • Guidance for Q3 includes an operating margin between 3% and 6% and a fuel cost per gallon of $2.61. Trousdale advised, "included in our Q3 revenue guide is a reduction of approximately 33% in other revenue versus our Q2 results, driven by a reduction of the number of aircraft we have on lease to unaffiliated airlines."

Financial Results

  • Sun Country reported Q2 total revenue of $263.6 million, up 3.6% year-over-year. GAAP pretax margin was 3.2% and adjusted pretax margin 3.9%. Passenger segment revenue was down 0.8% year-over-year, reflecting a 6.2% decline in scheduled service ASMs. Scheduled service TRASM increased 3.7%, with a total fare increase of 6.5%, offsetting a 1.3 percentage point decline in load factor. Charter revenue grew 6.4% to $54.3 million, and cargo revenue grew 36.8% to $34.8 million. Q2 charter block hours grew 7.9%, while cargo block hours increased 9.5% as the cargo fleet expanded to 15 aircraft by quarter-end. Adjusted CASM increased 11.3%, impacted by the decline in scheduled service ASMs and cargo ramp costs. Salaries rose 12.9%, driven by headcount increases and contractual rate hikes.
  • Total liquidity at the end of Q2 stood at $206.6 million, with total debt and lease obligations at $562 million. CapEx for 2025 is expected to be between $70 million and $80 million.

Q&A

  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley, asked about the path to $2.50 EPS and industry dependencies. Bricker responded the forecast uses "a general tailwind associated with inflation of about 3%" with a focus on normalized unit revenue and current fuel prices, indicating conservative assumptions.
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays, inquired about contract pricing step-ups. Trousdale confirmed, "We have an annual step up on the contract every -- basically on the anniversary of the contract," with Bricker adding, "current rates are much higher than they were this time last year...it just kicked in."
  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI, probed on margin improvement post-cargo ramp. Bricker stated, "I think fourth quarter for cargo ramp is pretty good. We're on track for that...the growth rate is going to be a little bit constrained as we absorb this cargo expansion."
  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank, questioned margin drag in Q3. Bricker estimated, "for the third quarter, you could do $10 million, 4% on pretax," and indicated bookings remain strong with "year-on-year monthly improvements in unit revenue."
  • Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen, discussed capital allocation and share buybacks. Bricker indicated a strategy balancing liquidity, asset deals, and potential shareholder returns, while Trousdale noted inflationary pressure on aircraft assets could affect share buybacks.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research, asked about Q3 and Q4 margin drags and recovery. Bricker predicted the margin drag is "peak impact" in Q3 and should "ameliorate over the fourth and first quarters," with "the next big opportunity...in March '26."
  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs, asked about lease extensions and geographic demand. Bricker explained lease extensions are due to both operator demand and the company's current inability to absorb fleet growth, and cited strong demand in the Northeast and Midwest, with weaker trends in Southern California and desert destinations.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone during the Q&A focused on near-term margin pressures, the cargo ramp's operational impact, and capital allocation, with questions often probing for clarity on timing, magnitude of cost drag, and growth pacing. The sentiment was neutral, with analysts referencing industry challenges and expressing interest in management's strategic flexibility and risk management.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Bricker stating, "We believe due to our structural advantages, we'll be able to reliably deliver industry-leading profitability throughout all cycles." In Q&A, the tone remained measured but transparent about operational headwinds, margin impacts, and cautious optimism on recovery as the cargo program matures.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence about medium-term targets is consistent, but the current quarter's tone acknowledges more acute short-term margin pressures and operational complexity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 2025 continues to prioritize cargo expansion, with scheduled passenger service volumes deliberately reduced versus Q2. The company projects a lower operating margin for Q3 (3%-6%) compared to Q2's reported margins.
  • Management's medium-term targets remain unchanged, reiterating the $1.5 billion revenue and $2.50 EPS for Q2 2027. The prior quarter emphasized the same cargo ramp and scheduled service pullback, but this quarter reflects more pronounced cost impacts and a sharper focus on the timing of recovery in scheduled service and margin improvement.
  • Analyst questions remain centered on the pace of cargo ramp, margin headwinds from underutilized passenger assets, and the timing of recovery. Management's tone has shifted to address short-term operational challenges more directly while maintaining confidence in long-term strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified short-term cost pressures from underutilized passenger assets, increased salaries, and temporary pullback in scheduled service, with "adjusted CASM will remain elevated as we do not anticipate to resume the growth of our scheduled passenger service until the back half of 2026."
  • The timing of cargo aircraft induction and pilot upgrades presents operational uncertainty.
  • Inflationary pressures on aircraft and engine assets could affect future capital allocation.
  • Analysts raised concerns about margin drag, the pace of recovery, and asset utilization.

Final Takeaway

Sun Country Airlines management reinforced their commitment to a diversified model and highlighted the near-term margin impacts from a rapid cargo fleet expansion. The company is focused on scaling cargo operations, managing cost pressures, and expects to restore scheduled service utilization through 2026. Management maintains medium-term financial targets of $1.5 billion in revenue and $2.50 in EPS by Q2 2027, emphasizing operational flexibility and balance sheet strength as key levers for future growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Umicore reports 1H results
data.description:
• Umicore press release (OTCPK:UMICF): 1H Revenues of € 1.8 billion.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of € 433 million and adjusted EBITDA margin of 24.3%.
  • Adjusted EBIT of € 302 million and adjusted EBIT margin of 17.0%.
  • Adjusted net profit (Group share) of € 135 million and adjusted EPS of € 0.56.
  • Umicore reiterates its guidance for the Battery Cathode Materials business unit, expecting adjusted EBITDA to be around break-even for 2025.
  • For the full Battery Materials Solutions Business Group, including henceforth Battery Recycling Solutions, adjusted EBITDA is forecasted between € -20 and € -25 million.
  • The outlook for 2025 includes an additional year-on-year EBITDA impact of at least € 100 million, originating from further efficiency improvements across the Group.

data.title: Ares targets record fundraising pace with over $46B YTD amid robust global demand and expanding real assets pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ares Management Corporation (ARES) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Michael J. Arougheti, CEO, reported "strong second quarter results, demonstrating the strength and resiliency of our business during periods of market volatility and the breadth and diversification of our growing global platform." He highlighted that Ares logged its "second highest quarterly fundraising total on record, of more than $26 billion raised with more than 20 strategies and 40 funds in market across three of our channels." Management indicated they are on pace to meet or exceed the previous year's record fundraising of $92.7 billion, with $46 billion in gross commitments raised year-to-date.
  • CEO Arougheti emphasized the expansion of the Real Assets Group, stating, "With nearly $130 billion in AUM and over 880 investment and operating professionals, our Real Assets Group is one of the largest managers of real estate and infrastructure assets across the globe, and we believe is very well positioned for greater scale and long-term growth."
  • He shared that AUM increased to $572 billion, driven by fundraising, deployment, and market appreciation, with fee-paying AUM reaching $350 billion. He added, "Our FPAUM increased to $350 billion, representing quarter-over-quarter organic growth of 17% on an annualized basis."
  • The wealth channel remains a strategic focus, with Ares reporting, "Our fundraising for the first half of the year totaling $7 billion in equity commitments, a 54% increase over the first half of 2024."
  • Arougheti noted, "Our net accrued performance income balance increased 8.5% in the quarter to $1.1 billion as we experienced strong investment results across our business."
  • CFO Jarrod Morgan Phillips stated, "Management fees were a record $900 million, representing a 24% year-over-year increase."
  • Phillips added, "The second quarter reflected our first full quarter of financials, including our GCP acquisition, which contributed $103 million in revenues and $34 million in FRE for a 33% FRE margin."

Outlook

  • Management expects to continue scaling fundraising into year-end, with CEO Arougheti stating, "We believe that we are in an excellent position to continue scaling our fundraise into year-end."
  • Phillips projected, "With the closing of our first data center fund totaling $2.4 billion, we expect to generate an additional $40 million in management leasing and development fees through the end of Q1 '26."
  • With perpetual capital AUM now at $167 billion, Arougheti stated, "We anticipate perpetual capital from both the wealth and institutional channels will continue to represent a significant percentage of our AUM growth going forward and should provide even greater visibility in revenue growth and profitability across the business."

Financial Results

  • CFO Phillips reported, "Fee-related earnings of $409 million for the quarter increased 26% year-over-year. FRE margins totaled 41.2% in the second quarter."
  • Phillips explained, "Our net realized performance income for the quarter was $16 million."
  • He described, "Overall, realized income totaled $398 million for the quarter, a 10% year-over-year increase."
  • Phillips noted, "Our effective tax rate on realized income was 9.5% which is in line with our range of 8% to 12% for the remainder of the year."
  • Phillips detailed, "In credit, we had quarterly gross returns of 5.5% for junior direct lending, 5.1% for opportunistic credit, 4.4% for our APAC credit strategy, 3% for alternative credit, 3% for U.S. senior direct lending and 2.2% for European direct lending."

Q&A

  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked about private credit institutional demand and fee compression. CEO Arougheti responded, "There's a lot of conversation in the market and the media just about the rapid growth in private credit...private credit fundraising institutionally is actually down sequentially for the last 3 years...Candidly, we have not really seen [fee pressure]."
  • William Raymond Katz, TD Cowen: Inquired about 401(k) market opportunities. CEO Arougheti replied, "We are big believers in the democratization of alternatives and increasing access to alternatives for the individual investor. We do feel like we are closer than ever."
  • Michael Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research: Asked about deployment pipelines. CEO Arougheti answered, "Everything that we're seeing now has us continuing to have confidence that the pipelines are building into Q3."
  • Kenneth Brooks Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about European private assets. CEO Arougheti stated, "We're seeing increased investment and we're seeing increased investor appetite...the performance within the private credit books are kind of right on top of each other [U.S. and Europe]."
  • Kyle Kenneth Voigt, KBW: Queried about retail distribution and international flows. CEO Arougheti said, "We continue to be incredibly excited about the progress we're making...the number of partnerships is increasing."
  • Benjamin Elliot Budish, Barclays: Asked about FRE margin outlook. CFO Phillips answered, "If we see a gangbusters back half of the year, deployment-wise, that will certainly be a boon to the margins...fundraising as it comes in from our nontraded products has an impact to our margins as well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was largely positive, probing for clarity around sustainability of fee rates, new market opportunities, and margin outlook. There was a focus on institutional and retail distribution, and the pace of deployment.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, with CEO Arougheti stating, "We believe that we have solidified our position as one of the top alternative managers of private market assets for individual investors and are poised to benefit as the wealth channel continues to allocate more capital into the asset class."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained constructive, with a greater emphasis on scale and new product initiatives. Analyst sentiment also remained positive, focusing more on forward growth opportunities and less on near-term risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fundraising momentum increased, with over $26 billion raised in Q2 compared to over $20 billion in Q1, and a year-to-date pace targeting or exceeding last year’s record.
  • AUM and fee-paying AUM grew to $572 billion and $350 billion, respectively, reflecting strong organic and acquisition-driven expansion versus the previous quarter’s $546 billion AUM.
  • Management fees increased to $900 million in Q2 from $818 million in Q1, with FRE margins holding steady despite GCP integration effects.
  • There was heightened focus on scaling perpetual capital and the real assets platform in Q2, while Q1 emphasized resilience amid volatility and early GCP integration.
  • Analysts remained focused on growth levers, but Q2 saw more questions around new product launches, international flows, and margin drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that "the deployment environment was modestly impacted, particularly at the beginning of the quarter," with a "temporary slowdown in transaction activity in April."
  • Potential delays in the timing of certain performance income realizations were referenced, with CFO Phillips noting, "following the market fluctuations, we experienced in the second quarter that may push out the timing of certain realizations."
  • Phillips highlighted, "integration of GCP temporarily compressed margins by 90 basis points," but remained confident about synergy realization.
  • Management stated that despite market volatility, "nonaccrual rates remain well below historical industry averages and our own historical averages."

Final Takeaway

Ares Management delivered robust second quarter results, supported by record fundraising, expanding market share in both institutional and wealth channels, and continued execution on real assets and perpetual capital strategies. Management maintains confidence in achieving or exceeding last year’s fundraising record, expects ongoing margin stability as integration synergies are realized, and sees strong momentum across major business units heading into the second half of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intel trades in red for seven straight sessions
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) continued losses for seven straight sessions as the stock closed 2.35% lower at $19.34 on Friday.

The Santa Clara, Calif.-based company lost around 12.50% in the last six trading sessions. INTC is down nearly 16% over the past one month. The stock closed 2.65% lower at $19.80 on Thursday.

Last month, Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) confirmed plans to spin off its networking and communications business as a standalone company.

Additionally, the semiconductor firm is planning to cut 15% jobs and plans to end the year with a global headcount of ~75,000 as a part of sweeping restructuring under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan.  As a part of the revamp, three senior executives in Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) manufacturing operations are going to retire, Reuters reported.

J.P. Morgan identified near-term risks, such as customer pull-in purchases falling off and gross margins continuing to drop below 40%. The bank reiterated its underweight rating on the stock but nudged the price target up to $21 from $20.

Morgan Stanley also said that gross margins will remain under pressure because of 18A startup costs and the Lunar Lake ramp. Morgan Stanley retained its Equal-weight rating and $23 price target.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts are cautious and rated the stock as Hold.

Intel shares have fallen above 4% so far this year, compared to a 7.8% gain in the broader benchmark index.

data.title: Cloudflare snaps six straight sessions of gains
data.description:
Cloudflare (NET) shares snapped six straight sessions of gains, as the stock closed 3.46% lower at $200.49 on Friday.

The company gained about 8.4% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock has gained more than 90% so far this year, compared to the nearly 7% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

NET is up nearly 9% over the past one month. The stock closed 3.4% higher on Thursday at $207.67.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, NET has a Hold rating with a score of 3.34 out of 5. The company received A in the prospect of momentum and a D in valuation and revisions factor.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

Seeking Alpha analysts Michael Del Monte noted, “Cloudflare's aggressive reinvestment and strong Cloudflare Workers adoption position it for long-term growth, especially in AI applications and large enterprise customers.”

Given the high valuation and long-term growth trajectory, Del Monte recommend a HOLD rating, advising caution for investors considering new positions.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 19 out of 34 analysts gave NET a Buy and above. 12 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and three recommended Sell or lower.

data.title: Fed's Kugler to depart governor post early on Aug. 8
data.description:
The Federal Reserve said Friday that Adriana Kugler will step down from her governor role, effective Aug. 8. Her term was slated to expire in January 2026.

Kugler will return to Georgetown University as a professor, she said, after serving as a Fed governor since Sept. 2023.

"It has been an honor of a lifetime to serve on the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System," Kugler said in a statement. "I am especially honored to have served during a critical time in achieving our dual mandate of bringing down prices and keeping a strong and resilient labor market."

The early resignation comes as U.S. President Donald Trump and his team have been constantly pushing for the central bank to lower interest rates this year. But the Fed has yet to budge, still holding rates steady.

The Trump administration is in the process of looking for someone to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term ends in next year's May.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has suggested the Trump administration could appoint someone to initially replace Kugler and subsequently promoting that person to the chair position.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Tech Voices: Palantir's $10B contract, Tesla verdict, Airbnb vs. NYC
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • The U.S. Army said it has signed a new Enterprise Agreement with Palantir worth up to $10 billion that "establishes a comprehensive framework for the Army's future software and data needs." 

The new EA will consolidate 75 contracts into a single contract, which will help reduce procurement time and "allow the government flexibility to purchase goods and services as needed," the Army said in a statement.

"This Enterprise Agreement represents a pivotal step in the Army's commitment to modernizing our capabilities while being fiscally responsible," said U.S. Army CIO Leo Garciga in the statement. "By streamlining our procurement processes and leveraging enterprise-level discounts, we are not only enhancing our operational effectiveness but also maximizing our buying power."

  • A Miami jury has found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) partly liable for a fatal car crash in 2019 that involved one of its Autopilot-equipped vehicles, awarding $329 million in damages to a crash survivor and the deceased victim's family. 

The accident occurred after the Tesla driver took his eyes off the road to pick up his cellphone, believing that the car's Enhanced Autopilot system would brake if it sensed any obstacles in the way. Instead, the car accelerated and hit two people on the side of the road, killing one, according to CNBC.

“Tesla designed Autopilot only for controlled access highways yet deliberately chose not to restrict drivers from using it elsewhere, alongside Elon Musk telling the world Autopilot drove better than humans,” the plaintiff's counsel, Brett Schreiber, told CNBC in an e-mailed statement on Friday. “Tesla’s lies turned our roads into test tracks for their fundamentally flawed technology, putting everyday Americans like Naibel Benavides and Dillon Angulo in harm’s way.”

  • Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) is optimistic that restrictions on its service in New York City will be lifted.

“Conversations are happening there, top-level conversations with the mayor and with mayoral candidates,” Airbnb CBO Dave Stephenson said in an interview with Bloomberg. “We’re having really good conversations about the importance of supporting the communities and the economics in those communities and I think that they’re realizing that the law is not serving the community well.”

Bloomberg noted that the company plans to spend around $10 million this year on local and state races to get politicians sympathetic to its cause elected. The company has already spent over $1 million on the New York City mayoral race.
